content.plain_english: This week your child is getting confident with numbers up to 100. They are reading and writing them in figures and words, and learning that the digits in a two-digit number stand for tens and ones — so 47 is four tens and seven ones.
content.in_school: Children start with practical resources — straws bundled into tens, base-ten blocks, or a long number line on the carpet. They then draw the same numbers as tens-and-ones diagrams, before writing them in figures, in words, and comparing them using <, > and =.
content.home_activity: On the walk to school this week, take turns calling out two-digit numbers you see — door numbers, bus numbers, prices in shop windows. Ask your child to tell you the tens digit and the ones digit. Then pick two of the numbers and ask which is bigger, and how they know. Five minutes is plenty.
dinner_table_questions: If I have 32 grapes and you have 47, how would you check who has more?
What is one more than 89? What is one less than 50?
Can you count up in fives from 30 until I say stop?

key_vocabulary.definition: The first digit in a two-digit number tells you how many groups of ten there are. In 47, there are 4 tens.
The last digit in a two-digit number tells you how many single units there are. In 47, there are 7 ones.
The idea that the position of a digit changes its value. The 4 in 47 is worth 40, not 4.
The symbol > means the number on the left is larger than the one on the right. 47 > 32.
The symbol < means the number on the left is smaller than the one on the right. 32 < 47.

example_questions: Write the number forty-six in figures.
What is the value of the 7 in 71 — is it 7 or 70?
content.times_table_tip: Year 2 children are working on instant recall of the 2, 5 and 10 times tables. Try counting in 5s on the way upstairs at bedtime — 5, 10, 15, 20.
